skill.name: coding-standards
skill.description: Universal coding standards, best practices, and patterns for TypeScript, JavaScript, React, and Node.js development.
skill.summary: Comprehensive coding standards guide with TypeScript, JavaScript, React, and Node.js best practices.
skill.author: affaan-m

security_audit.summary: This skill is a documentation resource containing coding standards and best practices in markdown format. The static analyzer flagged 72 potential issues, but all are false positives: JavaScript template literals in code examples were misidentified as shell execution, lazy loading patterns as dynamic imports, fetch examples as network calls, and generic error message text as cryptographic/system issues. No actual security risks exist - the skill provides educational content about code quality.

high_findings.title: False Positive: Shell Command Execution
high_findings.description: Static scanner flagged JavaScript template literals (backticks) as shell backtick execution. These are legitimate code examples showing template string syntax, not shell commands.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: All 53 'external_commands' findings are JavaScript code examples using template literals like `const x = 'value'`. No shell execution occurs in this documentation file.

high_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
high_findings.description: Static scanner incorrectly flagged text mentions in documentation as cryptographic algorithm usage. This is a coding standards guide, not executable code.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: No cryptographic algorithms are used or demonstrated. The scanner likely matched generic error message text like 'API key missing' and misidentified it.

medium_findings.title: False Positive: Dynamic Import
medium_findings.description: React lazy loading pattern example flagged as dynamic import security concern.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code example shows standard React lazy loading: `lazy(() => import('./HeavyChart'))`. This is a documented best practice, not a security issue.

medium_findings.title: False Positive: Network API Calls
medium_findings.description: Fetch API examples in documentation flagged as network access.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: These are educational code examples demonstrating proper error handling for fetch calls. They are documentation, not actual executable code making network requests.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Development terms like 'query parameters', 'filtering', and 'market data' incorrectly flagged as system reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Terms like 'query parameters' and 'filtering' refer to API design patterns in the documentation context, not system reconnaissance.

content.user_title: Apply Coding Standards to Your Code
content.value_statement: This skill provides comprehensive coding standards and best practices for TypeScript, JavaScript, React, and Node.js development, helping developers write clean, maintainable code.
actual_capabilities: Provides TypeScript and JavaScript coding standards with code examples
Shows React best practices including component structure, hooks, and state management
Documents API design conventions and response formats
Defines file organization and naming conventions for projects
Includes testing standards using the AAA pattern
Offers performance optimization tips including memoization and lazy loading
limitations: Does not execute code or perform automated linting
Does not integrate with specific IDEs or editors
Does not provide real-time code review

use_cases.title: Learn Modern JavaScript Patterns
use_cases.description: New developers can learn proper TypeScript and JavaScript coding patterns including immutability, async/await best practices, and type safety.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Code Style
use_cases.description: Development teams can adopt consistent coding standards across their projects with documented conventions for naming, formatting, and structure.

use_cases.title: Improve Code Review Process
use_cases.description: Use these standards during code reviews to ensure consistency and catch common code quality issues before merging.

prompt_templates.title: Ask for TypeScript Best Practices
prompt_templates.prompt: Using the coding-standards skill, show me the best practices for writing TypeScript interfaces and types.

prompt_templates.title: Get React Component Structure
prompt_templates.prompt: Using the coding-standards skill, what is the recommended structure for a React functional component with props?

prompt_templates.title: Request API Design Standards
prompt_templates.prompt: Using the coding-standards skill, document the REST API conventions and response format standards.

prompt_templates.title: Learn Error Handling Patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Using the coding-standards skill, show me the best practices for handling errors in async functions.

output_examples.input: Show me how to properly handle async errors
output_examples.output: Use try-catch blocks with proper error logging. Always throw meaningful errors rather than silently failing. For fetch calls, check response.ok and handle HTTP errors explicitly.

output_examples.input: What are the naming conventions for functions?
output_examples.output: Use verb-noun pattern like 'fetchUserData' or 'calculateTotal'. Avoid nouns alone like 'data' or 'handler'. For boolean functions, use 'is', 'has', or 'can' prefixes.

output_examples.input: How should I structure my React components?
output_examples.output: Use functional components with TypeScript interfaces for props. Define component props explicitly rather than using any type. Keep components focused on a single responsibility.

best_practices: Write self-documenting code with clear variable and function names instead of relying heavily on comments
Always use immutability patterns - prefer spread operators over direct mutation
Define proper TypeScript types rather than using 'any' to maintain type safety
anti_patterns: Avoid using 'any' type in TypeScript as it defeats the purpose of type checking
Do not mutate state directly - always use functional updates like setCount(prev => prev + 1)
Avoid deeply nested conditionals - use early returns to keep code flat and readable

faq.question: What programming languages does this skill cover?
faq.answer: This skill covers TypeScript, JavaScript, React, and Node.js development standards.

faq.question: Does this skill run linting on my code?
faq.answer: No, this skill provides documentation and guidance. It does not execute or analyze your actual code.

faq.question: Can I use this with Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes, this skill works with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code for AI-assisted coding guidance.

faq.question: Are these standards industry-accepted?
faq.answer: Yes, these standards follow widely-accepted patterns from the JavaScript ecosystem community.

faq.question: Does this include testing standards?
faq.answer: Yes, it covers test structure using the AAA (Arrange-Act-Assert) pattern and descriptive test naming.

faq.question: How often should I review these standards?
faq.answer: Use these standards as a reference during development and code reviews. Teams should adopt them as their baseline coding convention.
